responseSet.title: The Learning Process in Psychotherapy Supervision. By Imre Szecsödy. Stockholm.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Learning Process in Psychotherapy Supervision. By Imre Szecsödy. Stockholm.

Robert Hale

Robert Hale

Robert Hale  

Much is written about the need for training in psychotherapy; little attention is given in this country to studying the actual process of learning, particularly in the crucial area of the supervision of clinical work. Swedish psychotherapists and psychoanalysts have for some time recognised the need to train trainers. This book studies the interaction between supervisor and trainee in the course of supervision in the hope that it will inform and direct teaching methods.

It is essentially a report of a research study carried out in the Karolinska Institute in Stockholm, its purpose being to observe and clarify how learning takes place in the supervisory situation. It is in part descriptive and in part hypothesis-generating. Reading the excellent introduction and review of the literature on supervision, one realises how much of the work on supervision has come from the United States.

The research itself was carried out on four of the participants and trainers on a two-year psychotherapy training course. Treatment and supervision were both on a weekly basis, and each fifth supervision was recorded on audiotape and transcriptions, together with interviews with the participants, gave the date for the analysis which was carried out in two parts.

In the first part, an ‘educational diagnosis’ was made for each session by the use of what is described as a ‘micro-analytic’ technique; and an ‘ideal problem-solving route’ was formulated. The dialogue between trainee and supervisor was then followed, step by step, to see whether the actual supervisory interaction was conducted correspondingly or differently. Doubtless there would be many opinions on what constituted an ‘ideal route’.

The second part constituted a quantitative analysis of these same sessions. Sessions were divided into sequences which were judged and classified according to forty-eight variables, and then subjected to analysis using the Principal Component Method.

The results of the descriptive part of the study are of immediate relevance to us as supervisors.

[[RunningHead]]

The Visible and Invisible Group: Two Perspectives on Group and Group Process. By Yvonne Agazarian & Richard Peters. London: Routledge. £14.95 paperback. Pp. 292.

Sheena Grunberg

Sheena Grunberg

Sheena Grunberg  

There is, it seems, a problem in writing about psychotherapeutic groups in that most of the books on this subject are turgidly over-complicated and theoretically top-heavy, losing in the process the liveliness and power that those of us working in the field associate with group work. This phenomenon seems to derive from two sources. The first is the need to establish group psychotherapy as a serious psychotherapeutic discipline comparable to psychoanalysis. The second, and in my view most important, is the need to alleviate the anxieties of being overwhelmed by the complexity and primitive power of the group material for the conductors of groups. However, instead of holding fast to the experience of the group by remaining close to the clinical material to build up solid theoretical constructs, writers on this subject take flight into dehumanising theoretical concepts, diagrams, and complicated notational systems.

This book is of this genre, although it seems to have started out with the good intention of understanding the more personal and emotional influences in a group by stating that some comprehensive model of personality is essential for the effective use of group psychotherapy — in this case psychoanalysis.

Since Malan's work showed that the exclusive focusing on group dynamics in group psychotherapy denied the possibility of individual growth, making patients feel dehumanised, group therapists have been struggling with the problem of how to work on individual development through group process, knowing that a solution cannot be found by simply doing psychoanalysis of individuals in a group setting.

[[RunningHead]]

A One Day Scientific Meeting

Under the Auspices of the Anna Freud Centre and the Psychoanalysis Unit, University College, London in Honour of George S. Moran: 27 June 1992

Dr George Moran, Director of the Anna Freud Centre, died early this year from motor neurone disease. Over the years he contributed remarkably to the relevance of observations of early childhood development to psychoanalytic formulations and practice. There has been enormous progress in both fields, yet workers from these two disciplines have rarely had an opportunity to meet to discuss the implications of each field for the other.

This meeting, in honour of George Moran, will offer the rare opportunity to examine the common ground between recent progress in the study of human development and psychoanalytic theories. Leaders from both fields have agreed to participate to discuss their findings and ideas. They are Prof. Daniel Stern (Geneva), Prof. John Morton (London), Prof. Serge Lebovici (Paris), Mrs. Anne-Marie Sandler (London), Dr. Elizabeth Spillius (London), Prof. Donald Cohen (Yale), Dr. Peter Neubauer (New York), Prof. Al Solnit (Yale) and Dr. Peter Fonagy (London). The Chair will be taken by Prof. Joseph Sandler (London).

Admission is free but the number of places is limited. Those interested in attending must contact The “Conference Secretary, Psychoanalysis Unit, University College London, 26 Bedford Way, London WC1H OAP, telephone no. 071 580 6902 (answering machine), fax no. 071 289 4800, as soon as possible in order to ensure admission.

The meeting will take place at 9.00 am-6.00 pm at the Edward Lewis Theatre, Middlesex Hospital Medical School, Windeyer Building, 46 Cleveland Street, London W1. Refreshments will be available.

responseSet.title: John Derg Sutherland (25 April 1905-14 June 1991)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

John Derg Sutherland (25 April 1905-14 June 1991)

Anton Obholzer

Anton Obholzer

Anton Obholzer  

John Sutherland was Medical Director of the Tavistock Clinic between 1947 and 1968, and founder of the Scottish Institute of Human Relations.

Jock, as he was widely known, was the youngest of an Edinburgh family of eight. A studious child, he first read Chemistry, and then Psychology at Edinburgh University.

There he came under the influence of Ronald Fairbairn. Determined to become a psychoanalyst, he took his Medical degree at Glasgow University. At the outbreak of War in 1939 he joined the staff of a hospital for the expected psychiatric war casualties, and it was as a psychiatrist that he in 1942 joined a team of colleagues, predominantly from the Tavistock Clinic, to develop the War Office Selection Boards for Officers in World War II. He played a prominent part, not only in his actual contribution to the design, planning, and methodology, but in demonstrating significant leadership and influence within the professional/military group itself. This later lead to his being chosen as Director of the Tavistock Clinic, a post he held for twenty-one years (1947-1968). With his war-time colleagues, among whom were Henry Dicks, John Bowlby, and Wilfred Bion, he led the Clinic into its preeminent national and international position in psychoanalytically-oriented psychotherapy and related health endeavours. In 1948, he and his colleagues decided that the Clinic should play its part in the new National Health Service, and until his retirement in 1968 he strove successfully to establish the Clinic in its unique combination of postgraduate training and clinic practice — for children, adolescents, families, and adults — in group as well as individual treatment.

In 1968, in recognition of these services, the Queen bestowed upon him the CBE.

It was after the war that, while fulfilling his leadership role, conducting clinical work himself both for individuals and for groups, he continued his psychoanalysis training. As a member of the British Psycho-Analytical Society he played an equally prominent part, and later edited the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis for many years (1960-1968).

Jungian Child Psychotherapy: Individuation in Childhood. Edited by Mara Sidoli and Miranda Davies. Library of Analytical Psychology. £14.95 paperback.

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford  

This is a thoroughly useful and enjoyable book, which could be read with profit by most analytical therapists, whether they work with children or adults, and whether or not they are Jungians.

It consists of a collection of fifteen papers, some of which have been previously published in the Journal of Analytical Psychology; and these have in some cases been refined for the purposes of this book. The focus is Michael Fordham's important development of Jungian theory of the self and his fostering of child analysis. There are two papers by him. James Astor's thorough introduction outlines the theory, and the various authors emphasise different aspects in discussing the child cases they present. This makes for a rich experience for the reader. There are frequent drawings from infant-observation seminars, and the book as a whole demonstrates well how fertile the interaction between Jungian and Kleinian ideas has been. Winnicott and Bion, too, frequently inform the writers. I wished that Stern's work could have been referred to, but this book must have been in preparation, and most of the papers written, before The Interpersonal World of the Infant was published.

This collection should be on every Jungian's bookshelf. For other readers it will show not only how much modern Jungians in this country are indebted to object-relations theory, but also what a deep extra dimension they bring to it through Jungian archetypal understanding of unconscious processes.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Cases to Cases: A Study of Psychotherapy

Timo Niemi

Timo Niemi

Töölönkatu 37 A, 00260 Helsinki.

Timo Niemi  

The background to the study was a research project on psychotherapy (279 subjects) in the context of the Finnish Student Health Service. Patients were divided into three groups by duration of treatment. For each group, a hypothetical case was constructed. The hypothetical case in relation to short-term treatment (less than six months) is someone whose temporarily disturbed mental balance is supported by the therapist. The hypothetical case in relation to medium-term treatment (one to two years) exhibits recovery through release from self-objects. The hypothetical case in relation to long-term treatment (two to five years) was marked by the partial working through of object relationships.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Presenting the Case for NHS Psychotherapy Services—A Working Bibliography

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

London SE5 9RS

Jane Milton  

In the British National Health Service, many treatment units are now being called upon to justify their existence. Psychotherapy, as a specialty which is poorly resourced and ill-understood by outsiders, may encounter particular problems. Managers and planners are now increasingly contacting bodies such as the APP, the Tavistock Clinic, and the Psychotherapy Section of the Royal College of Psychiatrists, seeking information about psychotherapy, including data as to its efficacy. A conference was recently set up jointly by these organisations to address the problem of what sort of data to provide; and this bibliography originates from the conference. The author's aim is to provide a small working list of articles and books which could be used to inform the public and illustrate the multiple functions of psychotherapy units in the NHS, as well as to show evidence of the efficacy of the treatments provided.

Preventable Disasters: Pride, Shame, and Self-Blinding

Marcus Johns

Marcus Johns

120 Belsize Lane, London, NW3 5BA.

Marcus Johns  

The complaint about the safety conditions at an all-day conference in 1987 on preventable disasters is taken as the starting-point of discussion, both during that Conference and at a meeting afterwards, on the responses, typically irritation and denial, generated by warnings of disasters in general. These responses are linked to those of a child warned by his parents, as in the example of Tiresias's warning to King Oedipus in Sophocles's play, which illustrates such responses to warnings and reasons for them. It is suggested that an understanding of such reactions could lead to generally more effective responses to anxiety about possible dangers.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Children's Phantasies: The Shaping of Relationships By Otto Weininger. Karnac Books. Pp. 314. £14.95.

George Crawford

George Crawford

George Crawford  

In this book, Professor Weininger explores the unconscious phantasies which underlie and shape children's views of themselves and their relationships with others. It is written within the framework of psychoanalytic theory developed by Melanie Klein. The author makes a deliberate effort, not entirely successfully in my opinion, to clarify some key Kleinian concepts along the way.

In speaking of phantasies, he is referring to the child's inner world of assumptions, preoccupations, and expectations, which deeply affects and is affected by the impingement of external reality.

He provides many detailed descriptions of work with children in a variety of ordinary settings, such as play-groups and schools, as well as in more specialised play therapy and special-school settings. His descriptions are detailed and vivid. They convincingly convey the essence of a therapeutic interaction, ie the detailed observation not only of the child's use of the situation in terms of feelings and behaviour, but also the emotional response of the adult to what is being communicated.

The book will be useful to professionals working with children who have not themselves had training in therapeutic work from a psychoanalytical perspective. His clinical examples demonstrate the communicative richness of children's play.

When is a Year Not a Year? The Pressures Operating on a Trainee in Time-Limited Therapy

Mona Wilson

Mona Wilson

Harlesden Road, London, NW10 3RY.

Mona Wilson  

This paper presents an account of a trainee's experience of conducting one year of time-limited psychotherapy with an adult patient. It attempts to show some of the difficulties that were encountered by the trainee in this first experience of working with transference and countertransference phenomena. It also illustrates the pressures operating on a trainee offering time-limited psychotherapy and of how these were understood and worked with. The role of supervision and psychoanalytic concepts in managing these issues is discussed.

[[RunningHead]]

Pathological Narcissism and Addiction: A Self-Psychology Perspective

Eric Schoor

Eric Schoor

2728 Durant Avenue
BERKELEY California 94704.

Eric Schoor  

Following Kohut's schema of self-psychology, addiction may be considered as a form of pathological narcissism. Narcissistic transferences stemming from an archaic and fragile self are frequently observed in the addictions. Such patients typically experience others as an extension of the self. A clinical example of the emergence of ‘mirror’ and ‘idealising’ transferences is presented with a patient who was dependent upon alcohol. Kohut's insights on narcissism provide an approach to both comprehension and therapeutic intervention in enabling such patients to develop a more cohesive self.

[[RunningHead]]

Child and Adolescent Analysis: its significance for Clinical Work with Adults. Edited by Scott Dowling. New York: Int. Univ. Press. $330.50. Pp. xi + 232.

Joan Schachter

Joan Schachter

Joan Schachter  

This is the sixth in the series of workshop monographs published by the American Psychoanalytic Association. As in previous volumes, there are clinical papers followed by discussion papers, and a concluding chapter by the editor. This volume has a new section in which the authors respond to the discussants. I think this is a valuable addition, which enhances the fresh and lively quality of the book.

Scharfman points out in his introduction that it is commonly accepted that child and adolescent analytic work has had an impact on psychoanalytic conceptualisations and technique, though there have been few papers specifically on this subject. He refers to Anna Freud, Melanie Klein, Margaret Mahler, Erik Erikson, and Peter Blos in particular for their significant contributions to developmental theory. They have focussed our attention on pre-Oedipal development, examining the relationship between environmental and developmental factors in the course of analytic work and observation. It is useful to be reminded of how much these theories are now an integral part of adult analytic technique.

The three papers by child analysts emphasise the opportunity to observe developmental processes in statu nascendi, and the distorting influences of conflict. As an adult analyst, I wonder why we are not more positively encouraged to gain experience in child analysis, recognising the difference between the actual child and the child in the adult. The importance of discontinuities in development, seen more clearly in children, but existing too in adults, is emphasised by Abrahms. Chused focuses on the child analyst learning to listen to different levels of ego-functioning and to understand non-verbal behaviour. Though I think these issues have been written about extensively by adult analysts, particularly in work with borderline patients. (There are references throughout the book to what can be learnt about infantile states of mind in the child and in the borderline and psychotic patient.)

Forces of Destiny: Psychoanalysis and Human Idiom. By Christopher Bollas. London: Free Association Books. £27.50 hardback £12.95 paperback. Pp. 223.

Tom Murphy

Tom Murphy

Tom Murphy  

This is the author's second book, following two years after The Shadow of the Object. The book is in two sections, the first of which is a series of chapters describing two closely linked themes. The first theme concerns the analysand's development of parts of himself by making use of the analyst in a way which expresses the analysand's personal ‘idiom’. The second theme concerns the author's view that a person has a potential from birth which is probably specific enough to justify the term ‘destiny’, and that there is something like a drive to fulfil that destiny. However, a developing person needs receptive others for use, and in this area they may run into serious difficulty which adversely affects their development. The second part of the book is a collection of essays developing the ideas of the first half in a number of specific contexts. The clinical examples are clear and a much-needed anchor for the theorising, and although I would personally have found more clinical examples helpful I think the balance between the two is made acceptable by the readability of the prose style and by the additional vignettes from the author's own life.

To some extent the author is developing and extending on understanding first described by Winnicott, with the addition and further integration of work by Heimann, Klein, Bion, Lacan, and others. Much, however, is also original. I found Bollas's ideas and perspective thought-provoking in a way that brings new ideas to my own work. The style of the writing has an amiable quality which carries the reader along into ideas which are highly conceptual and require shifts of framework not reached by straightforward logical argument.

Leaving to one side the question of whether one agrees with the book's basic orientation, there is little to find fault with.

Feeling like a Good Psychotherapist—Or a Bad One: Critical Incidents in Psychotherapists' Experiences

Rolf Sandell , Per-Arne Rönnås and Johan Schubert

Johan Schubert

Rolf Sandell
Bjorngardsgatan 25, S-118 52 Stockholm.

Johan Schubert  

What makes a therapist feel competent or incompetent in a session, a concept related to the good — or bad — hour, is an implication of what the therapist believes is good therapy and therefore is trying to do — or believes is bad and therefore tries to avoid doing. In an attempt to infer covert processes in therapists, twenty-seven psychoanalytically-oriented psychotherapists furnished written accounts of sessions when they had felt like good — or bad — therapists. Accounts were analysed and interpreted to uncover the varieties of good and bad experiences and their grounds. Resistance and countertransference were found to be critical phenomena. When the therapist was able to cope with such complications, good feelings were generated, and when he was unable, bad ones — provided he became aware of this inability. If not, the therapist acted out patient-specific or unspecific countertransference feelings in ways that made him feel like a good therapist.

Alison Cookson

Alison Cookson  

This paper has arisen out of the understandings gained following the in-patient treatment of a severely disturbed and self-damaging young woman with a borderline personality disorder. The phenomenon of protective identification is considered in terms of its capacity affectively to control apparently separate and independent professional structures within a hospital setting so that an entire institution may become mobilised into reflecting and acting out one patient's internal world. This phenomenon is illustrated using examples from the patient's own art-work and by clinical vignettes which aim to highlight the destructively regressive processes which occurred. One of the greatest difficulties was found to be the seemingly inevitable loss of boundaries between self and object; between intrapsychic and interpersonal; between staff-member and patient. Consequently some thoughts are offered concerning the need for structures, practical and theoretical, which can be utilised to help recognise and understand the enormously complex and diverse events which such a situation so often engenders.

[[RunningHead]]

A Physically Handicapped Teenager is Helped to Own Her Body

Marta Smith

Marta Smith

21 Milnthorpe Drive, Wakefield, WF2 7HU

Marta Smith  

In this paper I am presenting my work with a 15-year-old girl, Nina, who was born premature with congenital feet deformities. Her twin had died at birth, and Nina spent eight weeks in a Special Care Baby unit. She had also suffered from bronchial asthma, which was under control during the months she was in therapy with me.

An attempt to overdose, and a letter she had written to a teacher, brought Nina to our services and to individual psychotherapy. The weekly sessions gave Nina the opportunity to elaborate her mourning for the dead twin and to face her physical problems more realistically. She had coped with these by idealising a beautiful body and giving it, in her phantasy, to her dead sister for whose death she felt responsible. Her identification with characters from horror stories, of which she was an avid reader, was a key to understanding how she felt trapped in her deformed body, to which she would refer in the phrase ‘It doesn't bother me.’ The working through of her feelings of guilt, anger, and envy enabled her to lessen the split and to own her body.

Summary of a Large-Scale Psychotherapy Study

Ursula Haug

Ursula Haug

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA.

Ursula Haug  

Preface

The large-scale outcome study (1004 patients) was carried out at the Institute of Psychogenic Illness in Berlin. It was originally published by Annemarie Dührssen in 1962. The present paper is a summary of the research.

The study was funded by the insurance company Allgemeine Ortskrankenkasse [AOK] in Berlin. Insurance cover is compulsory in Germany, and employers usually contribute to their employees’ insurance cover. In the case of the AOK, very low contributions are payable by employees. The unemployed are also covered by the AOK. Thus the AOK system of insurance in Germany seems very much like the British National Health Service. Dührssen's study is impressive, but the presentation of it is rather confusing. I have therefore not only summarised it but reorganised it into a more conventional shape. The other problem with the study is that it is rather ‘historical’ — the references are from the 1940s.

Introduction

This research study on the outcome of psychoanalytic psychotherapy was commissioned by the insurance company in order to establish the effectiveness of this kind of therapy. It was one paper in a series of research projects of this type, including not only data on therapy outcome, but also follow-up data.

Dührssen introduced the study with a list of the essential conditions that ought to be met by an outcome study. She listed six criteria:

The type of psychotherapy investigateted must be defined; and appropriate selection of patients for treatment as well as the standard of treatment must be ensured, patients must be selected appropriately; and the standard of treatment must be ensured.

The number of cases has to be large enough to ensure a statistically significant difference at evaluation.

The outcome statistics should be drawn as far as possible from the same setting. (Those from different settings cannot easily be compared.)

A statistically significant percentage of patients must be available for follow-up.

responseSet.title: The Values of Psychotherapy
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Values of Psychotherapy11

David Bell

David Bell

Richmond Surrey TW10 7JF

David Bell  

This is a wide-ranging book which will be of great interest to psychoanalytical psychotherapists — most especially those working in the public sector. It results from a fruitful collaboration between a psychotherapist (Holmes) and a philosopher (Lindley). It addresses fundamental issues that lie at the core of arguments concerning psychotherapy: there are chapters on the epistemological status of psychotherapy; the case for psychotherapy as a need and not a luxury; ethical issues in connection with the practice of psychotherapy; and the case for a psychotherapy profession.

It is the kind of book that psychotherapists might turn to to clarify their thinking, and also one which they might give to friendly managers who want to back up their case for expanding psychotherapy services. For this the authors are to be congratulated.

The core of the book rests upon the authors’ view that the central value of psychotherapy rests in its capacity to increase personal autonomy: freeing people from the internal factors that inhibit personal development. I think the authors present a strong case, although some of the philosophical details are not without their difficulties. Flynn (1990) has argued against the authors’ derivation of their ideas from the work of J S Mill (namely the utilitarian perspective).

In the first pages of the book the authors give a very broad definition of psychotherapy which covers psychoanalytic psychotherapy, family therapy, cognitive psychotherapy, and behavioural modifications — though the last seemed to me to be a bit forced. This broad definition serves to find a common denominator in all these approaches, but given the differences between these therapies, this common denominator is more of the lowest common denominator type. Flynn (namely the utilitarian perspective) has argued that the resemblances between therapies may be no more than ‘family resemblances’ (a term used by Wittgenstein)2. Whether the similarities between them ‘would be considered central or essential is a matter for further debate’ (Flynn). This breadth of definition has further difficulties. In the sections of the book concerned with the unjust distribution of psychotherapy services, and to some extent the sections dealing with general ethical questions, there is less of a problem. However, when the authors are dealing more specifically with theory and technique they muddy the waters by not distinguishing those aspects of psychoanalytic work that are specific to it.

[[RunningHead]]

Learning to Think about Schizoid Thinking

Murray Jackson

Murray Jackson

Le Pous
30630 St André-de-Roquepertuis
France.

Murray Jackson  

Psychoanalytic knowledge has much to contribute to the understanding and treatment of the seriously mentally ill. It can make apparently bizarre manifestations comprehensible, can facilitate the formulation and implementation of a psychotherapeutically-oriented treatment plan and can allow the selection of those individuals suitable for psychological treatments in general and psychoanalytic psychotherapy in particular.

All staff working with psychotic patients can be helped by acquiring some understanding of the nature of psychotic communication, and learning to think about schizoid and schizophrenic thinking could be regarded as a most important activity. It requires a hospital milieu where there is an interest in psychodynamics, staff who are actually wanting to undertake psychotherapeutic work with psychotic patients, and opportunity for supervision and discussion. Appropriate theoretical concepts are essential, and those of Klein and Bion are particularly helpful.

The author describes experience of such work in hospital settings in Scandinavia. All those participating learned how to begin to think about schizoid thinking, and to understand the nature of psychotic communication in greater depth. Ward staff appreciated the approach, and psychotherapists improved their skills, learning of the dangers of poor work with inappropriate patients, and of the benefit of careful work within a properly supportive context. Many patients who would not normally have been regarded as suitable for psychotherapy have been helped by this approach.

The Human Core: The Intrapsychic Base of Behavior. By Leo Rangell. Volume I: Action within the Structural View. Volume II: From Anxiety to Integrity. New York: International Universities Press, vol. I $60 Pp. x + 468; vol. II $60 Pp. x + 488 + index to the two volumes.

Carlos Fishman

Carlos Fishman

Carlos Fishman  

Leo Rangell, the well-known and prolific psychoanalyst from Los Angeles, has compiled thirty-five of his many papers in these two volumes. He attempts, subtly and in a thought-provoking manner to develop his psychoanalytic conception of what he calls the Human Core.

He aims to develop a theory of behaviour from a psychoanalytic perspective and launches himself on this not-inconsiderable challenge from his wide-ranging experience as clinician and teacher, as well as ex-President of the International Psycho-Analytical Association.

The thread that runs through this collection of papers is the attempt to study the role of ‘choice’ and ‘will’ (free will) within the bounds of psychic determinism. Previous attempts have constituted a difficulty for those theorists of psychoanalysis and psychotherapy (Fromm, Sullivan, Rogers, and others) who have tried to tackle the issue from different perspectives. Rangell's starting-point is firmly based in that stream of ego psychology that follows Hartmann and Rapaport in psychoanalysis.

In the first volume, Rangell describes micro and macro psychic processes — elements one could call them — of the totality of psychic experience, the organising of which he calls the Human Core. His first section contains mainly clinical papers which are used to illustrate the attention paid to intrapsychic processes in terms of anxiety, affect, and conflict. In the second section he goes on to elaborate the theories about these themes in which his two classic papers integrating Freud's two theories of anxiety are included, as well as his paper ‘A Psychoanalytic Theory of Affects’.

Finally, he comes to what is his original contribution, which deserves a great deal of thought as he attempts to describe the issue of the ego's unconscious ‘choice’ in dealing with anxiety aroused by a particular conflict. Rangell describes the forces that the ego has to contend with in making this choice, as well as the ‘process’ of choice and its corollaries: responsibility, autonomy, and further integrity. The particular importance in approaching these issues goes beyond his attempts at making a contribution to ‘Decision Making Theory’, which is his explicit interest. I think that there is scope for understanding that he is advancing some possibilities of comprehending the ‘mechanics’ of what Freud called the ‘choice of neurosis’. (We could add now the choice of perverse or psychotic organisations.)

Sandor Ferenczi: Reconsidering Active Intervention. By Martin Stanton. Free Association Books. £27.50 hardback £12.95 paperback. Pp. xv + 226.

Robert Whyte

Robert Whyte

Robert Whyte  

In this excellent book, Martin Stanton recommends study of Ferenczi on the grounds of his being one of the giants of the early psychoanalytic movement. The fact that he has been relatively little studied is attributed to a number of factors, including ‘character assassination’ by Ernest Jones, the assumption that Freud condemned his ‘active technique’, and the absence of a good, edited English translation of his work.

In the section entitled ‘Curriculum Vitae’, Stanton provides an historical background to Ferenczi's personal and professional development. The main body of the book describes Ferenczi's ideas. ‘Development’ can be considered as an analogy which displays Ferenczi's representation of the psychoanalytic process. The rest of the book is structure along the developmental lines of this analogy.

Firstly, there is an examination of how Ferenczi analyses time, and relates it to spatial progress. Then his ideas on transference are discussed, including the view that transference originates and progresses through play, this being linked with the inspiration of child analysis in Klein and Mahler. Ferenczi's views on child sex-abuse derive from his refusal to reduce the complex to the simple. Coitus, the declared aim of development, succeeds in Ferenczi's view at the level of continuation of the species, but fails to complete a return to primal unity.

Ferenczi criticises the over-valuation of theoretical insights to the detriment of ‘real, sincere sympathy’.

responseSet.title: Witnessing Psychoanalysis: From Vienna Back to Vienna via Buchenwald and the USA. By Ernst Federn.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Witnessing Psychoanalysis: From Vienna Back to Vienna via Buchenwald and the USA. By Ernst Federn.

Richard Cawthra

Richard Cawthra

Richard Cawthra  

This is a book written by Ernst Federn, son of Paul Federn, a pioneer within the psychoanalytic movement. It has eighteen chapters, grouped under four headings: (1) social psychology; (2) on the psychology of terror and violence; (3) a psychoanalytic psychotherapy; (4) on the history of psychoanalysis.

The wide range and variety of subjects covered throughout the work demonstrates a richness of experience which Federn conveys with enthusiasm for psychoanalysis, while managing to question and challenge some of its orthodoxy. His strength of character developed by experience within the psychoanalytic field shows through as Federn, and those whose ideas he quotes, refuse to be reduced by adversity, threat to life, or overpowering forces of resistance to understanding in psychoanalytic work. His account of his imprisonment in Dachau and Buchenwald during the war sets a tone for the book which is salutary and wise. Throughout, Ernst Federn often writes from a historical, social, or political perspective, yet he has contributed by giving us a book which is always clinically relevant as he tackles many topics of applied psychoanalysis which will be of interest to child, adolescent, and adult therapists.

I encountered in this book an honesty and frankness which made it easy to read. In addition to its subject matter it reminds us that something of the ‘person’, be it in his personality or character, is a vital force bigger than its component parts, which has enabled Ernst Federn to work within, and live out, psychoanalysis, while also being an observer of it and life.

I recommend this book as something different from the usual text. It gains by being of a different tradition. Federn's Viennese roots in psychoanalysis seem to get him nearer to the heart of the matter.

274

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychiatry without Walls: Some Psychotherapeutic Reflections

Jeremy Holmes

Jeremy Holmes

Raleigh Park, Barnstaple Devon, EX31 4JB.

Jeremy Holmes  

Community care, despite its many benefits, can result in fragmentation and confusion. Bion's and Bowlby's notions of containment and attachment are applied to the difficulties inherent in trying to practise psychotherapeutic psychiatry in a community setting. The treatment of a ‘difficult’ patient, which ended in suicide, is described. The implications for psychotherapy of the new arrangements in the National Health Service are discussed. Psychotherapeutic supervision for all mental health workers is essential if community care is to become effective, and this needs to be recognised by the development of adequately funded psychotherapy services.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Impediments to the Development of Attachment

Helen High

Helen High

7 Hillside Gardens
London, N6 5SU.

Helen High  

The function of the mother's reverie, as described by Bion, is viewed as a psychological parallel, following birth, to the physical function of the placenta.

Clinical material from psychotherapy with a 3 1/2-year-old girl illustrates that, as long as she maintained the delusion of being attached to her mother's body by a physical placenta, she was hindered from learning to experience herself as a separate individual and from developing normal feelings of attachment and grief over loss.

She was referred soon after her parents' separation when she showed a lack of reaction to the loss of her father. The material suggests that at the time of separation from her father she regressed to an earlier stage where there had already been a failure to work through infantile separation and individuation from her mother

The material reveals stages of her emergence from an illusory state of union, into one in which she could increasingly tolerate her separate existence. There followed an enrichment of her psychological means of communication through the use of imagination and symbolic play. She also became more receptive to her therapist's communications and could increasingly use psychological support to share and face inner pain.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Countertransference and Containment in a Patient with a Psychotic Mother

Sharon Hopkins

Sharon Hopkins

7 Summerlee Gardens
London N2 9QN.

Sharon Hopkins  

The importance of countertransference reactions in the treatment of a borderline patient with a psychotic suicidal mother is discussed. In such a case containment can be seriously disrupted by the violence of the patient's projections into the therapist. Where a psychotic internal object has been brought for treatment it is particularly important that the therapist be able to identify not only with the sane and mad parts of the patient but with the destructiveness and cruelty of the patient and her primary object. The difficulties of tolerating these relentless projections of madness and despair are increased by the multiple and confusing aims of the patient's excessive use of projective identification. The invasive and hostile aspects of this are often heavily disguised beneath idealization of the self and object as well as of madness itself. For the patient, attempts to interpret the idealization are moreover felt as an assault on the lost loved object, and the therapy then unconsciously becomes a persecutory experience for the patient. The therapist can thus be drawn into feeling the full force of the anger and hatred towards the real disappointing object and the sense of hopelessness about the reparative task. Although such feelings need to be tolerated, eventually some distance from the often-unpleasant fluctuations in the countertransference must develop if a useful of containment is to be achieved.

This paper follows the course of treatment of a child in therapy over a period of one year. Clinical material is used to illustrate the difficulties encountered in attempting to establish intensive psychoanalytic psychotherapy when the child's external environment was unstable. It is hoped that some thoughts arising out of this experience may serve as guidelines for possible application to other similar cases.

responseSet.title: The use of Brief Focal Psychotherapy in the Treatment of Chronic Pain
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The use of Brief Focal Psychotherapy in the Treatment of Chronic Pain

Judith Whale

Judith Whale

98 Cambridge Road, Great Shelford
Cambridge CB2 5JS.

Judith Whale  

Material is presented from brief focal psychotherapy used in the treatment of four patients with chronic pain refractory to multiple forms of treatment. The value from therapy for each patient is described in terms of actual pain relief, in improvement in understanding the interpretation of past trauma into present bodily symptoms, and in subsequent ability to cope with the pain. Three reported treatment to have improved their pain; one reported an improvement in her ability to cope with it and consequent improvement in lifestyle. The place of pathological mourning in the aetiology of such pain is considered, together with a discussion on the constant finding of denial or displacement of intense anger towards important past figures and its manifestation in the transference. A discussion of the place of brief focal psychotherapy as a time- and cost-effective intervention in the treatment of chronic pain is presented.

The Psychotic Personality: A Psychoanalytic Theory and its Application in Clinical Practice**

Richard Lucas

Richard Lucas

Woodford Bridge
Woodford Green, Essex, IG8 8BY.

Richard Lucas  

This paper presents and discusses aspects of Bion's theory of the psychotic personality. In particular, it focuses on the psychotic personality's attacks on the dependent and needy self. The paper considers how Bion's theory can be applied to understanding the schizophrenic patient. Examples are given to show its importance not only in analytic practice, but also within general psychiatry.

[[RunningHead]]

Notes towards a Psychoanalytic Understanding of the Couple Relationship

Stanley Ruszczynski

Stanley Ruszczynski

120 Belsize Lane, London, NW3 5BA

Stanley Ruszczynski  

In this paper an attempt is made to demonstrate how a particular orientation to contemporary psychoanalytic concepts may be useful to an understanding of the nature of the marital, or couple, relationship. The unconscious forces which may attract two people to each other, which may influence the patterns of their interaction and which may result in psychic stagnation or development are outlined. The second part of the paper addresses the nature of the therapeutic relationship with the patient couple. A hypothesis is offered regarding possible diagnostic criteria for choosing whether to work with a couple presenting for couple psychotherapy employing one psychotherapist or two psychotherapists working as a co-therapy pair. (The usual practice of myself and my colleagues at the Tavistock Institute of Marital Studies is to see our patient couples with a co-therapist partner).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Few Glances at Psychiatry

Tom Main

Tom Main

Tom Main  

This paper discusses the knowledge derived from weekly seminars with hospital psychiatrists, held by the author over a two-year period. Patients were selected by the psychiatrists from their own settings to be presented at the group, and material is presented from various transactions of the psychiatrists with these patients, and with the nursing staffln the hospital, to illustrate the particular constraints inherent in the hospital setting for doctors attempting to work psychotherapeutically. Five clinical examples are given of situations in which doctors had been led, by nursing staff, by the hospital system, or by the patient herself, to treat physical symptoms, or to treat with ECT, or to follow administrative requirements, in order to make the situations more comfortable, or to achieve immediate, though short-term, gratification. In this way the seminar provided a means of learning in the situation where more conventional ‘supervision’ had failed.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Jungian Sandplay: The Wonderful Therapy. By Joel Ryce-Menuhin. London: Routledge. £35 hardback £12.99 paperback.

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford  

Ryce-Menuhin's book describes the practice of sandplay — a therapy in which the patient, adult, or child, uses a sand-tray and objects (toys, figures, coloured bits and pieces) to express aspects of his inner world. The therapist sits with the patient, sketches the process, and after the session photographs the finished product.

It is a ‘classical’ Jungian therapy, in that the therapist's interpretations explore the personal and collective unconscious through active imagination and amplification, using alchemical, archetypal, and mythological motifs. It is not clear how much these interpretations are offered to the patient. Sandplays are made frequently by some patients, as the major aspect of their treatment, but the author's preference is to use sandplay ‘in conjunction with a long, deep, verbal Jungian analysis of many years’. He also offers sandplay sessions to the patients of other analysts, with special techniques to avoid transference conflicts in such cases. Unfortunately, he does not tell us what these are. Like many ‘classical’ Zurich Jungians, Ryce-Menuhin does not emphasise the transference/countertransference aspects of analysis, so that despite his four long clinical examples, one has little idea about the therapeutic relationship.

The author has some odd ideas about psychosomatics and ‘the involuntary nervous system’ (related, he says, to the archetypal image of the snake). He uses strong language to vilify some Freudian and Kleinian play therapists. But I liked his warning to fathers who want to compete with women in mothering babies, suggesting that they may have a pathological need to offer nurturing and that this may not be experience by the baby as good-enough care. A point worth making in these days of the NEW MAN.

Those who are interested in non-verbal forms of transference may enjoy this book; ordinary psychoanalytic psychotherapists are, I suspect, likely to be unimpressed.

185

[[RunningHead]]

Psychological Aspects of Stillbirth and Neonatal Death: An Annotated Bibliography. By S Bourne and E Lewis. Tavistock Clinic, Pp. 136. £10.

Dennis Carpy

Dennis Carpy

Dennis Carpy  

The title ably describes this excellent little book, which provides essential material for anyone looking at the area. The fact that there will be a readership for this book at all is largely due to the pioneering efforts of these authors, in the face of a happening, stillbirth, which ‘puts a heavy shadow across thought’.

In a historical introduction the authors describe the resistances they have encountered in themselves and others in the slowly developing professional awareness of the profound personal and family sequellae of these tragic events.

In the bibliography itself they have managed to gather and comment upon almost 400 writings on the subject, from about 1960 to the present day. Where suitable, they reprint the journal abstract of each article; otherwise they give a brief summary of their own. For the more important writings they add to this a critical comment on the item.

A book which I imagine will be of major help to a relatively small number of readers, but one which should certainly be held by libraries and be brought to the attention of those who work in the fields of obstetric and baby care.

272

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Freud/Jung Letters: The Correspondence between Sigmund Freud and C.G. Jung. Edited by William McGuire. Abridged by Alan McGlashan. Penguin. £7.99.

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford

Nicola Blandford  

This is a republishing by Penguin of Picador's 1989 abridged version of the Freud/Jung letters. The correspondence was edited by William McGuire and the abridgement is by Alan McGlashan. McGuire's lengthy introduction gives the story of how Freud and Jung came to correspond and also the fascinating tale of what happened to both sets of letters after the break in 1913: how they survived the ravages of European history; and the complex negotiations between representatives of both sides before it was finally agreed in 1980 that the correspondence could be published.

Altogether there survive about 360 letters. This abridgement omits some which are concerned with organisational details, and some which contain technical discussion. The purpose of this edition is to make available to a wide readership the documentary evidence of the relationship between Freud and Jung. Although there are longeurs at times, the letters are, of course, quite fascinating, and the build-up of tension in them as the relationship deteriorates is very powerful. The pain felt by each participant as he faced disillusionment about the other comes through very poignantly. So, too, does the distress of Emma Jung in the letters she wrote secretly to Freud in 1911 in an attempt to avert the rupture.

The analyst reader will probably want the unabridged version of this correspondence — the omissions in this edition are quite irritating at times. However, I think it is valuable to have this shortened version available in paperback. It can be recommended to trainees.

275

[[RunningHead]]

Dream, Phantasy and Art. By Hanna Segal. Routledge. Pp. 120

Victor Sedlak

Victor Sedlak

Victor Sedlak  

In this book Dr Segal expounds ideas that she has been working on for forty years. Those who know her published work will recognise many of her arguments, and indeed some of her clinical examples. And yet this book is much more than a re-working of her previous papers; it is an attempt to integrate and to form into a coherent whole those aspects of psychoanalytic theory and its application that she has studied for all of her professional life.

She writes in her introduction that her aim is to make her book interesting enough for psychoanalysts and yet accessible to the more general reader. With this aim in mind, she begins by describing Freud's theory of dreams, and then moves on to the theme of unconscious phantasy in order to acquaint the reader with the concepts and the subject matter that she will develop in future chapters. I found these first two chapters rather condensed and despite the lucidity and the excellent use of clinical examples, I think they would be rather hard work for a general reader with little background knowledge. They are invaluable to those who already have some grounding in the concepts, in that her strength is in her ability to integrate the concepts, to show how they interrelate, and to demonstrate how she has developed them to form the bedrock out of which come her orientations to her clinical work and to her understanding of the production of art.

Chapter 3 is based on her seminal 1957 paper on symbolism, and is followed by a development of her 1979 postscript to that paper. In this she uses Bion's concept of the container and the contained to understand more deeply the development of a capacity to symbolise. And she goes further in linking this to the early Oedipal situation and its working through. Throughout, her emphasis is on the psychic work that such working through involves and on the mental capacities that are its consequence. Segal demonstrates the difference between a mind that is capable of symbolic communication and alpha functioning and one that has to employ a more primitive means of evacuation of beta elements in her discussion of the different functions of dreaming. Her insight that dreams may be a means of ridding the mind of accretions of stimuli rather than the product of dream work is a most useful one in the clinical situation.

responseSet.title: Darkness Visible—and the Springs of Creativity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Darkness Visible—and the Springs of Creativity11

Christopher Cordess

Christopher Cordess

16 Highlever Road London W10 6PS

Christopher Cordess  

This book is essentially an essay on melancholia, and in particular an account of the profound illness into which the author sank — aged 60 the first time. The illness was characterised by ‘a state of anxiety, agitation, and unfocussed dread’, in which at one point he recalls

I could not rid my mind of the line of Baudelaire, skittering round at the edge of my consciousness, ‘I have felt the wind of the wing of madness’.

William Styron was previously best known, perhaps, for his novel, and the subsequent film, Sophie's Choice. His first novel, Lie down in Darkness, made much of the metaphor of darkness, not only in its title, and, in Darkness Visible, Styron tells us that in retrospect he wonders if there were not premonitions of the darkness of this later psychotic depressive breakdown, for long held at bay from much earlier in his life. There have been other novels in between, including The Long March, Set this House on Fire, and The Confessions of Mat Turner.

I found this essay so powerful that my first reaction was that it had brought me nearer an understanding of the subjective experience of depression than had any professional text. On second reading, I felt puzzled, for Styron evades more personal issues than he directly makes explicit; the power, I conclude, lies not in the overt content so much as in the capacity to evoke an awful experience.

Styron writes of other writers, for example William James,

who battled depression for many years, [and] gave up the search for an adequate portrayal, implying its near-impossibility when he wrote ‘It is a positive and active anguish, a sort of psychical neuralgia wholly unknown to normal life’.

I was moved to write to Styron, and sufficiently stimulated by his largely negative experience of the capacity of psychiatry and psychotherapy adequately and urgently to respond to his predicament, to include with my letter a copy of Eliott Jaques's ‘Death and the Mid-Life Crisis’, of which as I read Styron I had been much reminded. Jaques's classic paper explores human creativity in general and, in particular, the creativity of artists in relation to their psychological development, specifically to the mid-life crisis and the ‘depressive position’ as described by Melanie Klein.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Alienation and Perversion and Hidden Selves: Between Theory and Practice in Psychoanalysis. By M Masud, R Khan. Maresfield Library. Karnac. £12.95 each paperback. Pp. 245 and 204 respectively.

Victor Sedlak

Victor Sedlak

Victor Sedlak  

These books, first published in 1979 and 1983 respectively, have now been republished. I have chosen to review them together since I believe that my experience in reading them will be shared by other readers and applies equally to both volumes. It is an experience dominated by the question ‘What could have happened to this man, who can write so brilliantly and show such intuition, that his personality deteriorated so drastically in the last tragic years of his life?’ It is not perhaps a laudable question to pose, but it is one that is difficult to avoid.

The book on perversion has as its central thesis the assertion that the pervert is alienated from himself and his object by his exclusive and never-ending focus on the intricacies of his perversion to the exclusion of his other needs and other, human, attributes of his object. There is much clinical material to support this formulation and the author describes many variations on this theme. However, I was left uncomfortable with Khan's almost total emphasis on the failure of the environment, specifically Mother, in the formation of a perversion. There is very little, if any, consideration of the infant's consitutional capacity to tolerate frustration and to accept the confines imposed by reality. The existence of a Father is barely acknowledged, and the question must remain whether this absence might, in some cases, be due to the pervert's own difficulty in tolerating the psychic reality of Father and the primal scene.

The second book is most accessible and contains some fascinating case material. A valuable idea in its concerns the provision of an analytic space in which the patient can be accommodated in the state in which he presents himself, and without asking him to adapt to the analyst's requirements. Although it can demand huge practical resources to do this (Khan employed a houseboy and a secretary, and was willing to extend sessions, to see patients late at night, etc.), there is a principle in this which is important to all clinical work, and it is the fundamental one of respect for the patient. Khan is very aware of the danger of making the patient’ feel shamed or blamed, and of the masochistic uses to which this can be put. However, there does seem to be an implication that, given this space, the patient's hidden self that will emerge will be good. I thought that in a number of the clinical examples there was a failure to analyse the destructive forces in the patient, with the consequence that important ego functions of self-care were never safely established. Indeed, at times Khan seems to have fulfilled these functions himself, on the patient's behalf. One case I would exclude from this criticism is the one he entitled ‘The Evil Hand’, which I thought was a masterly presentation of extraordinary clinical work.
